session.assistant: Phase 4 done. Now Phase 5: Add remote path tests. Let me also check any callers of `validate_objective` to see if the signature change will break other call sites.
session.tool_result: tests/unit/cli/commands/objective/test_check_cmd.py:441: result = validate_objective(fake_gh, Path("/fake/repo"), 700) tests/unit/cli/commands/objective/test_check_cmd.py:453: result = validate_objective(fake_gh, Path("/fake/repo"), 999) docs/learned/cli/objective-commands.md:32:The `validate_objective()` function returns a discriminated union — either `ObjectiveValidationSuccess` or `ObjectiveValidationError`. This allows callers to distinguish between "couldn't validate" (network error, issue not found) and "validation completed but found problems" (orphaned statuses, inconsistent numbering). docs/learned/cli/objective-commands.md:127:- [Discriminated Union Error Handling](../architecture/discriminated-union-error-handling.md) - Result type patterns used by `validate_objective()` docs/learned/objectives/tripwires.md:23:**adding a new validation check** → Read [Roadmap Validation Architecture](roadmap-validation.md) first. Structural checks go in parse_roadmap() and return warnings alongside data. Semantic checks go in validate_objective() and produce pass/fail results. Don't mix levels. docs/learned/objectives/tripwires.md:65:**modifying roadmap validation without understanding the two-level architecture** → Read [Roadmap Validation Architecture](roadmap-validation.md) first. Validation is split between parse_roadmap() (structural) and validate_objective() (semantic). Read this doc to understand which level your change belongs in. docs/learned/objectives/tripwires.md:69:**raising exceptions from validate_objective()** → Read [Objective Check Command — Semantic Validation](objective-roadmap-check.md) first. validate_objective() returns discriminated unions, never raises. Only CLI presentation functions (\_output_json, \_output_human) raise SystemExit. docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:14: - action: "raising exceptions from validate_objective()" docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:15: warning: "validate_objective() returns discriminated unions, never raises. Only CLI presentation functions (_output_json, _output_human) raise SystemExit." docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:27:The roadmap parser in `erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.roadmap` (`packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/metadata/roadmap.py`) handles **structural** validation: can the markdown be parsed into phases and steps? The check command's `validate_objective()` in `check_cmd.py` adds a **semantic** layer: is the parsed roadmap internally consistent? docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:60:This matters because callers need different responses for "issue doesn't exist" vs "issue exists but has problems." Only the CLI presentation layer (`_output_json`, `_output_human`) converts results to exit codes and `SystemExit` — the core `validate_objective()` function is designed for programmatic use and never raises. docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:71:**WRONG: Raising exceptions from `validate_objective()`.** docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:9: warning: "Validation is split between parse_roadmap() (structural) and validate_objective() (semantic). Read this doc to understand which level your change belongs in." docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:11: warning: "Structural checks go in parse_roadmap() and return warnings alongside data. Semantic checks go in validate_objective() and produce pass/fail results. Don't mix levels." docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:28:**Semantic validation** (in `validate_objective()`) answers: "Is this roadmap internally consistent?" It only runs during `erk objective check` and produces pass/fail check results. It depends on structural parsing succeeding first — if no phases parse, semantic checks are skipped and the command returns early. docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:45:The semantic checks in `validate_objective()` enforce invariants that connect the status system to the PR column: docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:71:This is why `validate_objective()` returns `ObjectiveValidationError` (couldn't even start) vs `ObjectiveValidationSuccess` (ran checks, some may have failed) — the discriminated union separates "validation couldn't run" from "validation ran and found problems." docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:81:- Semantic validation: `validate_objective()` in `check_cmd.py` docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-validation.md:93:- `validate_objective()` adds check: `"Roadmap: none (objective has no roadmap)"` and **skips checks 3-7** (all roadmap-dependent), returning early with `passed=True` docs/learned/objectives/objective-lifecycle.md:168:This distinction matters for `validate_objective()` in `check_cmd.py`: when no roadmap block exists, it reports "Roadmap: none (objective has no roadmap)" as a passing check and skips checks 3-7 (all roadmap-dependent). The `erk objective view` command displays "No roadmap data found" for roadmap-free objectives. docs/learned/objectives/objective-lifecycle.md:195:| `check_cmd.py` / `validate_objective()` | Read-only | `erk objective check` | N/A (reads only) | Inherits from `parse_roadmap()` | src/erk/cli/commands/objective/check_cmd.py:106:def validate_objective( src/erk/cli/commands/objective/check_cmd.py:303: result = validate_objective( src/erk/cli/commands/objective/plan_cmd.py:140: result = validate_objective(ctx.issues, repo.root, issue_number) src/erk/cli/commands/objective/plan_cmd.py:215: result = validate_objective(ctx.issues, repo.root, issue_number) src/erk/cli/commands/objective/plan_cmd.py:737: result = validate_objective(ctx.issues, repo.root, issue_number) src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/objective_save_to_issue.py:214: validation_result = validate_objective(github, repo_root, result.plan_number) src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/objective_plan_setup.py:81: validation_result = validate_objective(github_issues, repo_root, objective_number)

1→"""Save plan as objective GitHub issue. 2→ 3→Usage: 4→ erk exec objective-save-to-issue [OPTIONS] 5→ 6→This command extracts a plan and creates a GitHub issue with: 7→- erk-objective label only (NOT erk-plan - objectives are not plans) 8→- No title suffix 9→- Plan content directly in body (no metadata block) 10→- No commands section 11→ 12→Options: 13→ --session-id ID: Session ID for scoped plan lookup 14→ --format: json (default) or display 15→ --validate: Run objective validation after creation 16→ 17→Exit Codes: 18→ 0: Success - objective issue created 19→ 1: Error - no plan found, gh failure, etc. 20→""" 21→ 22→import json 23→from pathlib import Path 24→from typing import Any, cast 25→ 26→import click 27→ 28→from erk.cli.commands.objective.check_cmd import ( 29→ ObjectiveValidationError, 30→ ObjectiveValidationSuccess,

1→"""Fetch, validate, and set up context for objective-plan command. 2→ 3→Consolidates data fetching, validation, marker creation, and roadmap 4→serialization into a single exec command, eliminating the Task agent. 5→ 6→Usage: 7→ erk exec objective-plan-setup 3679 --session-id SESSION_ID 8→ 9→Output: 10→ JSON with objective data, roadmap, validation results, and marker status. 11→ 12→Exit Codes: 13→ 0: Success 14→ 1: Error (not found, wrong label, validation error) 15→""" 16→ 17→import json 18→from pathlib import Path 19→ 20→import click 21→ 22→from erk.cli.commands.objective.check_cmd import ( 23→ ERK_OBJECTIVE_LABEL, 24→ ObjectiveValidationError, 25→ validate_objective, 26→) 27→from erk_shared.context.helpers import require_issues, require_repo_root 28→from erk_shared.gateway.github.issues.abc import GitHubIssues 29→from erk_shared.gateway.github.issues.types import IssueNotFound 30→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.dependency_graph import phases_from_graph

session.assistant:
I can use `require_context` to get the `ErkContext`, then use `get_remote_github` and resolve owner/repo. For the exec scripts, the approach is: 1. `require_context(ctx)` to get the `ErkContext` 2. `get_remote_github(erk_ctx)` to get the `RemoteGitHub` 3. Get owner/repo from `erk_ctx.repo.github`
